summary: Patrick McKenzie recounts his experience at the Business of Software 2010 conference, synthesizing lessons from speakers including Seth Godin, Dharmesh Shah, Eric Ries, Scott Farquhar, Jason Cohen, Paul Kenny, Rob Walling, Peldi from Balsamiq, Eric Sink, Dan Bricklin, Derek Sivers, and Joel Spolsky. The talks covered themes from tribal marketing and customer happiness metrics to acquisition mechanics and the ethical responsibilities of founders. McKenzie weaves his own insights throughout, particularly around A/B testing, charging more, and the compounding nature of career experience. Joel Spolsky's closing argument — that founders have ethical obligations in how they deploy the leverage their businesses create — left a lasting impression on McKenzie, challenging his view that keeping a company small is a morally neutral lifestyle choice.
key_points: Technical competence is no longer scarce; marketing, community-building, and creating movements around software are now the key differentiators
Customer Happiness Index (CHI) as a predictive churn metric can be exploited across sales, product, and policy decisions to dramatically improve retention
Permission-based email marketing with autoresponder sequences is the highest-leverage channel for converting visitors into customers over time
Overnight success is a myth — Peldi's Balsamiq hockey stick disappears when you zoom out to include decades of skill-building
Founders have ethical responsibilities about what their businesses contribute to the world, not just personal lifestyle optimization
money_quotes: As the cost of software approaches zero, you want to be the guy who owns the machine.
Absence of churn does not indicate the presence of delight.
Don't make awesome software, make awesome superstars. Make your customers awesome at their jobs.
If you work while they sleep, they won't know that you're ignoring them.
The most interesting people you will ever meet are the people who are most interested in you.
Customer faith in your product as a solution to their problem is directly proportional to how well they believe that you understand their problem.
You literally needed an entire lifetime to prepare for what you're doing today.
key_insight: The most important assets in a software business are no longer technical — they are community, trust, and the ethical choices founders make about how to deploy the leverage their businesses create.
